replies.text:
I use to be able to say that the things me and my geeky friends liked were something I was proud to have sponsored with my patronage. Now, I can no longer say that. As feminists have taken over, franchises, industries, genres are being decimated and the original customer base removed, in favor of a tiny demographic that the current sub culture has become enamored with. Which may have been their aim all along. Women already comprise upwards to 80% of the consumer base in most areas. What will happen when a tiny demographic, because it will be very small, feels that the cultural icons of the current day are theirs to control and keep? Well, we're seeing that now. 

Will it continue. The answer is yes, because finances is not their aim, their goal is control.

replies.author: @markbrown2206
replies.text:
Just in case anyone is here that didn't understand the Orville. Seth MacFarlane couldn't get the backing to write a Star Trek like show. So, he pitched the Orville as a Star Trek parody. Season 1 he had to include the jokes because the money men were watching the scripts (but he snuck in a love for Star Trek). But when Season 1 turned in a profit they just handed him the cash and told him to do it again. So after season 1 the jokes are toned down and the real love letter to Star Trek could begin (although there are still jokes and the odd misstep).
So, if for some reason you haven't seen The Orville, go watch it now. You can thank me later.

replies.author: @Gothmetalhead13
replies.text:
​ @kiritotheabridgedgod4178  ​ @samblack5313  Alrighty.
Firstly, the paintjob, Before we even get into the rest of it, the paintjob. Look at any, and I mean any, military vehicle with an enclosed interior, from an armoured car to a battleship to a jet. What colour is the interior painted? 99.9999999% of the time its either white (or an off white) or a pale green. Why? Because they are lighter colours that reflect light better and improve overall vision inside the compartment, plus they also help mitigate negative mental health affects that can occur when people are shoved into an enclosed space. What colour is the bridge in this show? Its black/really dark grey, with accents of a darker red. Not only does that screw with visibility, but it will also result in mass depression amongst the bridge crew and utterly destroy performance.

Secondly, the lighting. Again, I am going to point towards military vehicles. Their interiors are usually well lit with a red light reserved for operational conditions owing to how the red colour spectrum works. And I am going to say this now, you WANT your operational area to be well lit as often as possible. On a spaceship, especially one with an enclosed bridge, you are going to want good lighting. Good lighting boosts visibility, helps limit eye strain from screens, and is beneficial to mental health. This bridge is about as well lit as a nightclub, and coincidentally I am pretty sure thats where they got those awful lights that, from what I have seen in the trailer, seem to turn on and off in sequence, which is utterly retarded. The bridge crew are going to be suffering mental health issues, eye strain, and be stumbling around in the dark. Gods forbid someone drops something thats important, because it is GONE.

And then we have the layout. Sure, it follows the classic Federation layout, the captains chair is in the centre, except unlike on the other shows where the bridge is relatively compact and the captain has instant and easy access to any station, with the two arguably most vital ones - helm and gunnery - directly infront of him where he can easily communicate with them, observe, or take over if someone becomes a casualty and there is no immediate replacement, this chair - or should I say Command Throne - is set right in the centre of a massive empty dais. With rails around it, presumably to prevent the plebs from approaching the glorious majesty of Xers Most Federious Navies Captain. Someone has taken the wrong ques from Warhammer 40K. But they have no ability to directly interact with any of the stations, and if something happens to the helm or gunnery officers then they will have to take precious seconds - seconds that could see the ship destroyed, pacing across the dais, through the gap in the pleb fence, and down the ramp, to intervene. They will also have a harder time communicating with the rest of the crew whilst under fire or during an emergency as they will be shouting across dead space, and I know it may not seem much, but in a chaotic situation when consoles are exploding, casualties are screaming, and people are shouting to be heard over the noise, its going to make the Captains job of restoring order that bit harder.
That pleb fence also presents another problem. It blocks off a good chunk of the centre of the bridge, meaning the crew cannot just take the most direct route to another station if, say, the Helm officer gets KO'd, or the Shield Control station or Sensor station Officer gets taken out. Instead they have to loop around the bridge's perimeter, again taking precious seconds extra to get to the station that needs them, something that, again, could lead to unnecessary damage to the ship. It also presents a problem when it comes to treating or evacuating casualties, as medical staff cannot take the most direct route, and the casualties then have to be carried around the long loop to the exit.
The Captain, for some reason, has a spotlight positioned directly above them. I have no idea why anyone would ever do this as not only will the constant glare cause eye pain for the poor bugger (and if they have light sensetivity like me they will really suffer) but it will also cause them to be uncomfortably hot. Lights of that kind put out a lot of heat when on. I would not like to be sat directly under one.
I also want to touch on the sensor layout screens/readouts/whatever they are. From what I can see in the photos they are off to one side of directly behind the Helm and Gunnery/Sensor stations. This means that in order to look at them the Captain has to swivel their chair away from those stations and, presumably, the main screen (all previous Federation vessels have the  main screen here) and take their focus away from the operation of the vital functions of the ship. It means that in action the Captain is constantly having to look around, or swivel their chair, to view what could be critical information. In just about every ship I can think of, the Captains position is at the back, where they can have all the stations, officers operating those stations, and any data screens, within their field of view, allowing them to gather any needed data with a glance. In the case of this flying disaster the Captain has to either have 360 vision or be constantly swivelling.

Finally, theres the issue of space. On a warship you always have limitations, and in the case of Federation vessels its internal mass. The ship is only so big, and you have a lot of components that need to be crammed into a warships hull (I am assuming this is intended as a warship as it is a Starfleet vessel, although the garden does make me wonder) and any changes to one will affect the other. You want a bigger engine to go faster? Cool, but now theres less space for crew amenities so the crew suffer discomfort and moral takes a hit. Want more firepower? You can install more guns and torpedo tubes (projectors?) but as a result you have less space for storage, so your operational range is limited. Want to have a crazy fancy garden space for your crew? Thats fine but you had to strip out backup systems, so you have less redundancy in the event of your ship taking damage.
Simply put, the bridge is massive, far bigger than it needs to be. Its so large that it actually negatively impacts the operation of the ship, as mentioned above, but this also means it is taking up space that could be used for other components, as well as making the Bridge itself a larger target and thus more likely to be hit in combat. Yes, its not much, but on a warship every little bit of space/mass counts, and you have to wonder what sacrifices where made elsewhere in construction so the Captain could have the budget Imperium of Man Navy Captain experience.
Thats a rough overview. Its been a 10 hour day and now its gone midnight and I have another early start coming up. Hope it helped.

It was a fun ride; great comedies, brilliant independent movies, a time before I knew every single thing that movie stars had on their minds. I’m just gonna go back and watch the things I enjoyed. There’s zero hope these days. They’ve ruined everything and I’m tired of getting upset over it.

I’m gonna go watch Ghostbusters now.

author: @ConceptJunkie
text:
My wife and I watched the DS9 premiere the other night, which I hadn't seen in 10 years or more, and I was struck with how well it was written.  I do remember watching with her and a bunch of friends when it first aired.  They did a whole lot of world-building in that episode, way more than any other Trek show premiere needed, and still managed to introduce a whole bunch of characters who were played by really good actors, especially Avery Brooks, who brought so much calm dignity and strength to the role while giving us a much different take on the "captain" role.  I really enjoyed it, because I had kind of forgotten how good Star Trek used to be.

Star Trek might be dead and rotting, but I'll always have my DVDs.

replies.author: @calmingtrekcommentaries7008
replies.text: ​@ronricherson6685ok ok. I'll give you a more considered response. The reason I say Starfleet is not a military organisation is that is what Gene Roddenberry said. Starfleet's primary mission is exploration. A military's primary mission is defence. Yes, they use naval ranks, they have ships and weapons. Starfleet does have a defence role and you do need to have organisation and a chain of command in a very large organisation. Equating Starfleet with a military (in the 21st century sense) does an injustice to Gene's Vision of a peaceful future focused on exploration and the improvement of the human condition.
